Probabilistic PCA

Prerequisites

This chapter builds on Multivariate Normal and Probabilistic Graphical Models. The EM algorithm and Gibbs sampling used for inference are formalized in the Expectation Maximization and MCMC chapters (Part III); the versions presented here are self-contained introductions.

High-dimensional data often has far less intrinsic variation than the dimension DDD would suggest. A class of studentsŌĆÖ grade transcripts, or a collection of images, may vary along just a few underlying ŌĆ£axes.ŌĆØ Latent variable models make this intuition precise by positing that the high-dimensional observations xnŌłłRD\mbx_n \in \reals^DxnŌĆŗŌłłRD are generated from a low-dimensional latent variable znŌłłRM\mbz_n \in \reals^MznŌĆŗŌłłRM with MŌē¬DM \ll DMŌē¬D.

In this chapter we study the simplest such models, where the relationship between zn\mbz_nznŌĆŗ and xn\mbx_nxnŌĆŗ is linear and all distributions are Gaussian:

  • Principal Components Analysis (PCA): two classical optimization formulations and their connection to the eigenvectors of the sample covariance matrix

  • Probabilistic PCA (PPCA): PCA as the maximum likelihood solution to a Gaussian latent variable model, enabling Bayesian inference

  • Factor Analysis (FA): a generalization of PPCA with per-dimension noise variances

  • Other linear LVMs: Independent Components Analysis (ICA) and Probabilistic Canonical Correlation Analysis (PCCA)

Source
import torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Principal Components Analysis┬Č

Motivation┬Č

Suppose we observe grade transcripts {xn}n=1N\{\mbx_n\}_{n=1}^N{xnŌĆŗ}n=1NŌĆŗ where xnŌłłRD\mbx_n \in \reals^DxnŌĆŗŌłłRD is a vector of DDD grades for student nnn. We might want to:

  • Reduce dimensionality: are there a few axes of variation?

  • Visualize: embed the points in 2ŌĆō3 dimensions for plotting.

  • Compress: summarize each studentŌĆÖs record compactly.

PCA addresses all three. There are two classical equivalent formulations.

Maximum Variance Formulation┬Č

Goal: project the data onto an MMM-dimensional subspace while maximizing the variance of the projected data.

For M=1M = 1M=1, let u1ŌłłRD\mbu_1 \in \reals^Du1ŌĆŗŌłłRD be a unit vector defining the subspace. The projection of xn\mbx_nxnŌĆŗ is the scalar u1ŌŖżxn\mbu_1^\top \mbx_nu1ŌŖżŌĆŗxnŌĆŗ (the score or embedding). Its variance over the NNN data points is:

1NŌłæn=1N[u1ŌŖżxnŌłÆu1ŌŖżx╦ē]2=u1ŌŖżSu1,\begin{align} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^N \bigl[\mbu_1^\top \mbx_n - \mbu_1^\top \bar{\mbx}\bigr]^2 = \mbu_1^\top \mbS \mbu_1, \end{align}N1ŌĆŗn=1ŌłæNŌĆŗ[u1ŌŖżŌĆŗxnŌĆŗŌłÆu1ŌŖżŌĆŗx╦ē]2=u1ŌŖżŌĆŗSu1ŌĆŗ,ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(1)

where x╦ē=1NŌłænxn\bar{\mbx} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_n \mbx_nx╦ē=N1ŌĆŗŌłænŌĆŗxnŌĆŗ is the sample mean and S=1NŌłæn(xnŌłÆx╦ē)(xnŌłÆx╦ē)ŌŖż\mbS = \frac{1}{N} \sum_n (\mbx_n - \bar{\mbx})(\mbx_n - \bar{\mbx})^\topS=N1ŌĆŗŌłænŌĆŗ(xnŌĆŗŌłÆx╦ē)(xnŌĆŗŌłÆx╦ē)ŌŖż is the sample covariance matrix.

Maximizing u1ŌŖżSu1\mbu_1^\top \mbS \mbu_1u1ŌŖżŌĆŗSu1ŌĆŗ subject to Ōłźu1Ōłź2=1\|\mbu_1\|^2 = 1Ōłźu1ŌĆŗŌłź2=1 via a Lagrange multiplier gives Su1=╬╗1u1\mbS \mbu_1 = \lambda_1 \mbu_1Su1ŌĆŗ=╬╗1ŌĆŗu1ŌĆŗ, so u1\mbu_1u1ŌĆŗ must be an eigenvector of S\mbSS. Left-multiplying by u1ŌŖż\mbu_1^\topu1ŌŖżŌĆŗ shows the projected variance equals ╬╗1\lambda_1╬╗1ŌĆŗ, so we should choose the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue.

More generally, the MMM-dimensional principal subspace is spanned by the MMM eigenvectors u1,ŌĆ”,uM\mbu_1, \ldots, \mbu_Mu1ŌĆŗ,ŌĆ”,uMŌĆŗ with the largest eigenvalues ╬╗1ŌēźŌŗ»Ōēź╬╗M\lambda_1 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_M╬╗1ŌĆŗŌēźŌŗ»Ōēź╬╗MŌĆŗ.

Linear Autoencoder Formulation┬Č

Goal: find an orthogonal matrix WŌłłRD├ŚM\mbW \in \reals^{D \times M}WŌłłRD├ŚM (with WŌŖżW=IM\mbW^\top \mbW = \mbI_MWŌŖżW=IMŌĆŗ) that minimizes mean squared reconstruction error.

We encode as zn=WŌŖż(xnŌłÆx╦ē)\mbz_n = \mbW^\top (\mbx_n - \bar{\mbx})znŌĆŗ=WŌŖż(xnŌĆŗŌłÆx╦ē) and decode as x^n=Wzn+x╦ē\hat{\mbx}_n = \mbW \mbz_n + \bar{\mbx}x^nŌĆŗ=WznŌĆŗ+x╦ē. The loss is:

L(W)=1NŌłæn=1NŌłźxnŌłÆx^nŌłź2=Tr[(IŌłÆWWŌŖż)S(IŌłÆWWŌŖż)]=Tr[S]ŌłÆTr[WŌŖżSW].\begin{align} \mathcal{L}(\mbW) &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^N \|\mbx_n - \hat{\mbx}_n\|^2 = \Tr\bigl[(\mbI - \mbW\mbW^\top)\mbS(\mbI - \mbW\mbW^\top)\bigr] = \Tr[\mbS] - \Tr[\mbW^\top \mbS \mbW]. \end{align}L(W)ŌĆŗ=N1ŌĆŗn=1ŌłæNŌĆŗŌłźxnŌĆŗŌłÆx^nŌĆŗŌłź2=Tr[(IŌłÆWWŌŖż)S(IŌłÆWWŌŖż)]=Tr[S]ŌłÆTr[WŌŖżSW].ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(2)

Minimizing L(W)\mathcal{L}(\mbW)L(W) is equivalent to maximizing Tr[WŌŖżSW]=Ōłæm=1M╬╗m(wmŌŖżum)2\Tr[\mbW^\top \mbS \mbW] = \sum_{m=1}^M \lambda_m (\mbw_m^\top \mbu_m)^2Tr[WŌŖżSW]=Ōłæm=1MŌĆŗ╬╗mŌĆŗ(wmŌŖżŌĆŗumŌĆŗ)2, which is again solved by W=UM\mbW = \mbU_MW=UMŌĆŗ, the leading MMM eigenvectors.

PCA and the Singular Value Decomposition┬Č

Let Y=1NXc\mbY = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \mbX_cY=NŌĆŗ1ŌĆŗXcŌĆŗ where Xc\mbX_cXcŌĆŗ has rows (xnŌłÆx╦ē)ŌŖż(\mbx_n - \bar{\mbx})^\top(xnŌĆŗŌłÆx╦ē)ŌŖż. Then YŌŖżY=S\mbY^\top \mbY = \mbSYŌŖżY=S.

The SVD of Y=V╬ø1/2UŌŖż\mbY = \mbV \mbLambda^{1/2} \mbU^\topY=V╬ø1/2UŌŖż reveals:

  • Right singular vectors of Y\mbYY (columns of U\mbUU) = eigenvectors of S\mbSS = principal components.

  • Squared singular values = eigenvalues ╬╗1,ŌĆ”,╬╗D\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_D╬╗1ŌĆŗ,ŌĆ”,╬╗DŌĆŗ of S\mbSS.

In practice, PCA is computed via SVD of Y\mbYY rather than by eigendecomposing S\mbSS, since SVD is numerically more stable.

Explained Variance┬Č

The fraction of total variance captured by MMM principal components is:

variance┬Āexplained=Ōłæm=1M╬╗mŌłæd=1D╬╗d.\begin{align} \text{variance explained} = \frac{\sum_{m=1}^M \lambda_m}{\sum_{d=1}^D \lambda_d}. \end{align}variance┬Āexplained=Ōłæd=1DŌĆŗ╬╗dŌĆŗŌłæm=1MŌĆŗ╬╗mŌĆŗŌĆŗ.ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(3)

A scree plot shows this quantity (per-component and cumulative) as a function of MMM. Below we generate synthetic data from a rank-3 linear Gaussian model and visualize the scree plot.

# Generate synthetic data from a rank-3 linear Gaussian model
torch.manual_seed(305)
N, D, M_true = 300, 20, 3   # datapoints, features, true latent dims
W_true = torch.randn(D, M_true)         # D x M weight matrix
Z_true = torch.randn(N, M_true)         # N x M latent variables
Žā2     = torch.tensor(0.5)              # noise variance
X = Z_true @ W_true.T + torch.sqrt(Žā2) * torch.randn(N, D)

# Centre the data and compute PCA via SVD
╬╝  = X.mean(0)                          # sample mean, shape (D,)
Xc = X - ╬╝                              # centred data, shape (N, D)
Y  = Xc / N**0.5                        # Y.T @ Y = sample covariance S
_, s, Vt = torch.linalg.svd(Y, full_matrices=False)
UM  = Vt.T                              # principal components, columns of UM
╬╗   = s**2                              # eigenvalues of S (variances)
Source
fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 4))

pct = 100 * ╬╗ / ╬╗.sum()
axs[0].bar(range(1, D+1), pct.numpy(), color='steelblue', alpha=0.8)
axs[0].set_xlabel('Principal component')
axs[0].set_ylabel('Variance explained (%)')
axs[0].set_title('Per-component variance')

axs[1].plot(range(1, D+1), torch.cumsum(pct, 0).numpy(), 'o-', color='steelblue')
axs[1].axhline(90, color='r', ls='--', lw=1, label='90%')
axs[1].set_xlabel('Number of components')
axs[1].set_ylabel('Cumulative variance explained (%)')
axs[1].set_title('Scree plot')
axs[1].legend()

plt.tight_layout()

Probabilistic PCA┬Č

Model┬Č

The two classical formulations above cast PCA as an optimization problem. Probabilistic PCA (PPCA) instead defines a joint distribution over xn\mbx_nxnŌĆŗ and a latent variable zn\mbz_nznŌĆŗ:

znŌł╝iidN(0,IM)xnŌłŻznŌł╝N(Wzn+╬╝,ŌĆģŌĆŖŽā2ID),\begin{align} \mbz_n &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_M) \\ \mbx_n \mid \mbz_n &\sim \cN(\mbW \mbz_n + \mbmu,\; \sigma^2 \mbI_D), \end{align}znŌĆŗxnŌĆŗŌłŻznŌĆŗŌĆŗŌł╝iidN(0,IMŌĆŗ)Ōł╝N(WznŌĆŗ+╬╝,Žā2IDŌĆŗ),ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(4)

where WŌłłRD├ŚM\mbW \in \reals^{D \times M}WŌłłRD├ŚM are the loadings (analogous to the principal components), ╬╝ŌłłRD\mbmu \in \reals^D╬╝ŌłłRD is the mean, and Žā2\sigma^2Žā2 is an isotropic noise variance.

Equivalently, xn=Wzn+╬╝+ŽĄn\mbx_n = \mbW \mbz_n + \mbmu + \mbepsilon_nxnŌĆŗ=WznŌĆŗ+╬╝+ŽĄnŌĆŗ with ŽĄnŌł╝N(0,Žā2ID)\mbepsilon_n \sim \cN(\mbzero, \sigma^2 \mbI_D)ŽĄnŌĆŗŌł╝N(0,Žā2IDŌĆŗ).

The marginal distribution of xn\mbx_nxnŌĆŗ is:

p(xnŌłŻW,╬╝,Žā2)=N(xnŌłŻ╬╝,ŌĆģŌĆŖWWŌŖż+Žā2IDŌÅ¤C),\begin{align} p(\mbx_n \mid \mbW, \mbmu, \sigma^2) = \cN(\mbx_n \mid \mbmu,\; \underbrace{\mbW\mbW^\top + \sigma^2 \mbI_D}_{\mbC}), \end{align}p(xnŌĆŗŌłŻW,╬╝,Žā2)=N(xnŌĆŗŌłŻ╬╝,CWWŌŖż+Žā2IDŌĆŗŌĆŗŌĆŗ),ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(5)

a Gaussian with low-rank plus diagonal covariance ŌĆö only O(MD)O(MD)O(MD) parameters instead of O(D2)O(D^2)O(D2).

Maximum Likelihood Estimation┬Č

The marginal log-likelihood is:

L(W,╬╝,Žā2)=ŌłÆND2logŌüĪ2ŽĆŌłÆN2logŌüĪŌłŻCŌłŻŌłÆ12Ōłæn=1N(xnŌłÆ╬╝)ŌŖżCŌłÆ1(xnŌłÆ╬╝).\begin{align} \mathcal{L}(\mbW, \mbmu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{ND}{2}\log 2\pi - \frac{N}{2}\log|\mbC| - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^N (\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top \mbC^{-1} (\mbx_n - \mbmu). \end{align}L(W,╬╝,Žā2)=ŌłÆ2NDŌĆŗlog2ŽĆŌłÆ2NŌĆŗlogŌłŻCŌłŻŌłÆ21ŌĆŗn=1ŌłæNŌĆŗ(xnŌĆŗŌłÆ╬╝)ŌŖżCŌłÆ1(xnŌĆŗŌłÆ╬╝).ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(6)

Tipping and Bishop (1999) showed the MLE has a closed form:

╬╝ML=x╦ē,WML=UM(╬øMŌłÆŽā2I)1/2R,ŽāML2=1DŌłÆMŌłæm=M+1D╬╗m,\begin{align} \mbmu_{\mathsf{ML}} &= \bar{\mbx}, \\ \mbW_{\mathsf{ML}} &= \mbU_M (\mbLambda_M - \sigma^2 \mbI)^{1/2} \mbR, \\ \sigma^2_{\mathsf{ML}} &= \frac{1}{D - M} \sum_{m=M+1}^D \lambda_m, \end{align}╬╝MLŌĆŗWMLŌĆŗŽāML2ŌĆŗŌĆŗ=x╦ē,=UMŌĆŗ(╬øMŌĆŗŌłÆŽā2I)1/2R,=DŌłÆM1ŌĆŗm=M+1ŌłæDŌĆŗ╬╗mŌĆŗ,ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(7)

where UM\mbU_MUMŌĆŗ contains the MMM leading eigenvectors of the sample covariance S\mbSS, ╬øM=diag(╬╗1,ŌĆ”,╬╗M)\mbLambda_M = \diag(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_M)╬øMŌĆŗ=diag(╬╗1ŌĆŗ,ŌĆ”,╬╗MŌĆŗ), and RŌłłRM├ŚM\mbR \in \reals^{M \times M}RŌłłRM├ŚM is an arbitrary orthogonal matrix.

The weights are identifiable only up to orthogonal rotation ŌĆö only the subspace spanned by UM\mbU_MUMŌĆŗ is uniquely determined. The MLE noise variance ŽāML2\sigma^2_{\mathsf{ML}}ŽāML2ŌĆŗ is the average variance discarded in the trailing DŌłÆMD - MDŌłÆM dimensions.

Posterior Distribution on Latent Variables┬Č

Given parameters W\mbWW, ╬╝\mbmu╬╝, Žā2\sigma^2Žā2, the posterior on zn\mbz_nznŌĆŗ combines the Gaussian prior with the Gaussian likelihood:

p(znŌłŻxn,W,╬╝,Žā2)=N(znŌłŻJŌłÆ1hn,ŌĆģŌĆŖJŌłÆ1),\begin{align} p(\mbz_n \mid \mbx_n, \mbW, \mbmu, \sigma^2) = \cN(\mbz_n \mid \mbJ^{-1}\mbh_n,\; \mbJ^{-1}), \end{align}p(znŌĆŗŌłŻxnŌĆŗ,W,╬╝,Žā2)=N(znŌĆŗŌłŻJŌłÆ1hnŌĆŗ,JŌłÆ1),ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(8)

where

J=IM+1Žā2WŌŖżWŌłłRM├ŚM,hn=1Žā2WŌŖż(xnŌłÆ╬╝)ŌłłRM.\begin{align} \mbJ &= \mbI_M + \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \mbW^\top \mbW \in \reals^{M \times M}, \qquad \mbh_n = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \mbW^\top (\mbx_n - \mbmu) \in \reals^M. \end{align}JŌĆŗ=IMŌĆŗ+Žā21ŌĆŗWŌŖżWŌłłRM├ŚM,hnŌĆŗ=Žā21ŌĆŗWŌŖż(xnŌĆŗŌłÆ╬╝)ŌłłRM.ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(9)

Note that the posterior precision matrix J\mbJJ is the same for all nnn; only the information vector hn\mbh_nhnŌĆŗ varies. The M├ŚMM \times MM├ŚM system is much cheaper to invert than the D├ŚDD \times DD├ŚD marginal covariance.

Zero-noise limit: as Žā2ŌåÆ0\sigma^2 \to 0Žā2ŌåÆ0, the posterior mean converges to (WŌŖżW)ŌłÆ1WŌŖż(xnŌłÆ╬╝)(\mbW^\top \mbW)^{-1} \mbW^\top (\mbx_n - \mbmu)(WŌŖżW)ŌłÆ1WŌŖż(xnŌĆŗŌłÆ╬╝), the ordinary least-squares projection ŌĆö identical to classical PCA (up to scaling).

# MLE for PPCA with M components
M_fit  = 3
UM_fit = Vt[:M_fit].T              # D x M leading principal components
╬øM     = torch.diag(╬╗[:M_fit])    # M x M eigenvalue matrix
Žā2_ML  = ╬╗[M_fit:].mean()         # MLE noise variance

# MLE weights (orthogonal rotation R = I)
W_ML = UM_fit @ (╬øM - Žā2_ML * torch.eye(M_fit)).sqrt()

# Posterior on latent variables
J     = torch.eye(M_fit) + (1/Žā2_ML) * W_ML.T @ W_ML   # M x M precision
╬Żz    = torch.linalg.inv(J)                              # M x M posterior cov
H     = Xc @ W_ML / Žā2_ML                               # N x M info vectors
Z_hat = H @ ╬Żz.T                                         # N x M posterior means

print(f'MLE noise variance Žā┬▓  = {Žā2_ML:.3f}  (true = {Žā2.item():.3f})')
print(f'Posterior covariance ╬Żz:\n{╬Żz.numpy().round(3)}')
MLE noise variance Žā┬▓  = 0.483  (true = 0.500)
Posterior covariance ╬Żz:
[[ 0.024  0.    -0.   ]
 [ 0.     0.038  0.   ]
 [-0.     0.     0.082]]
Source
# Visualize the 2D posterior mean embedding
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 5))
ax.scatter(Z_hat[:, 0].numpy(), Z_hat[:, 1].numpy(),
           c=Z_true[:, 0].numpy(), cmap='coolwarm', s=20, alpha=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel(r'$\hat{z}_1$ (posterior mean)')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$\hat{z}_2$ (posterior mean)')
ax.set_title('PPCA posterior mean embeddings')
plt.tight_layout()

Gibbs Sampling for Probabilistic PCA┬Č

If we want a fully Bayesian treatment, we place a prior on the parameters. For simplicity assume ╬╝=0\mbmu = \mbzero╬╝=0 (center the data first) and put:

p(W,Žā2)=ŽćŌłÆ2(Žā2ŌłŻ╬Į0,Žā02)ŌłÅd=1DN(wdŌłŻ0,ŌĆēŽā2╬║0IM),\begin{align} p(\mbW, \sigma^2) = \chi^{-2}(\sigma^2 \mid \nu_0, \sigma_0^2) \prod_{d=1}^D \cN(\mbw_d \mid \mbzero,\, \tfrac{\sigma^2}{\kappa_0} \mbI_M), \end{align}p(W,Žā2)=ŽćŌłÆ2(Žā2ŌłŻ╬Į0ŌĆŗ,Žā02ŌĆŗ)d=1ŌłÅDŌĆŗN(wdŌĆŗŌłŻ0,╬║0ŌĆŗŽā2ŌĆŗIMŌĆŗ),ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(10)

where wdŌłłRM\mbw_d \in \reals^MwdŌĆŗŌłłRM is the ddd-th row of W\mbWW.

Complete conditional for each row wd\mbw_dwdŌĆŗ: combining the row prior with the NNN likelihoods for the ddd-th output gives a Gaussian:

p(wdŌłŻ{zn,xn},Žā2)=N(wdŌłŻJdŌłÆ1hd,ŌĆģŌĆŖJdŌłÆ1),\begin{align} p(\mbw_d \mid \{\mbz_n, \mbx_n\}, \sigma^2) = \cN(\mbw_d \mid \mbJ_d^{-1} \mbh_d,\; \mbJ_d^{-1}), \end{align}p(wdŌĆŗŌłŻ{znŌĆŗ,xnŌĆŗ},Žā2)=N(wdŌĆŗŌłŻJdŌłÆ1ŌĆŗhdŌĆŗ,JdŌłÆ1ŌĆŗ),ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(11)

where

Jd=╬║0Žā2IM+1Žā2Ōłæn=1NznznŌŖż,hd=1Žā2Ōłæn=1Nxn,dŌĆēzn.\begin{align} \mbJ_d &= \frac{\kappa_0}{\sigma^2} \mbI_M + \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{n=1}^N \mbz_n \mbz_n^\top, \qquad \mbh_d = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{n=1}^N x_{n,d}\, \mbz_n. \end{align}JdŌĆŗŌĆŗ=Žā2╬║0ŌĆŗŌĆŗIMŌĆŗ+Žā21ŌĆŗn=1ŌłæNŌĆŗznŌĆŗznŌŖżŌĆŗ,hdŌĆŗ=Žā21ŌĆŗn=1ŌłæNŌĆŗxn,dŌĆŗznŌĆŗ.ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(12)

Complete conditional for Žā2\sigma^2Žā2: conjugate with the scaled inverse chi-squared family:

p(Žā2ŌłŻ{zn,xn},W)=ŽćŌłÆ2(Žā2ŌłŻ╬ĮN,ŽāN2),╬ĮN=╬Į0+DM+DN,ŽāN2=1╬ĮNŌĆēŌüŻ[╬Į0Žā02+╬║0ŌłźWŌłźF2+Ōłæn=1NŌłźxnŌłÆWznŌłź2].\begin{align} p(\sigma^2 \mid \{\mbz_n, \mbx_n\}, \mbW) &= \chi^{-2}(\sigma^2 \mid \nu_N, \sigma_N^2), \\ \nu_N &= \nu_0 + DM + DN, \\ \sigma_N^2 &= \frac{1}{\nu_N}\!\left[\nu_0 \sigma_0^2 + \kappa_0 \|\mbW\|_F^2 + \sum_{n=1}^N \|\mbx_n - \mbW \mbz_n\|^2\right]. \end{align}p(Žā2ŌłŻ{znŌĆŗ,xnŌĆŗ},W)╬ĮNŌĆŗŽāN2ŌĆŗŌĆŗ=ŽćŌłÆ2(Žā2ŌłŻ╬ĮNŌĆŗ,ŽāN2ŌĆŗ),=╬Į0ŌĆŗ+DM+DN,=╬ĮNŌĆŗ1ŌĆŗ[╬Į0ŌĆŗŽā02ŌĆŗ+╬║0ŌĆŗŌłźWŌłźF2ŌĆŗ+n=1ŌłæNŌĆŗŌłźxnŌĆŗŌłÆWznŌĆŗŌłź2].ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(13)

Complete conditional for zn\mbz_nznŌĆŗ: same as the PPCA posterior derived above.

Connection to Bayesian linear regression

Each row update p(wdŌłŻ{zn,xn,d},Žā2)p(\mbw_d \mid \{\mbz_n, x_{n,d}\}, \sigma^2)p(wdŌĆŗŌłŻ{znŌĆŗ,xn,dŌĆŗ},Žā2) is exactly Bayesian linear regression ŌĆö predict scalar xn,dx_{n,d}xn,dŌĆŗ from features zn\mbz_nznŌĆŗ with a Gaussian prior on coefficients wd\mbw_dwdŌĆŗ. The Gibbs sampler alternates between updating the ŌĆ£regressorsŌĆØ (zn\mbz_nznŌĆŗ, which play the role of features) and the ŌĆ£weightsŌĆØ (W\mbWW).

Factor Analysis┬Č

Factor analysis (FA) relaxes the isotropic noise assumption of PPCA by allowing each output dimension to have its own noise variance:

znŌł╝iidN(0,IM)xnŌłŻznŌł╝N(Wzn+╬╝,ŌĆģŌĆŖdiag(Žā2)),\begin{align} \mbz_n &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_M) \\ \mbx_n \mid \mbz_n &\sim \cN(\mbW \mbz_n + \mbmu,\; \diag(\mbsigma^2)), \end{align}znŌĆŗxnŌĆŗŌłŻznŌĆŗŌĆŗŌł╝iidN(0,IMŌĆŗ)Ōł╝N(WznŌĆŗ+╬╝,diag(Žā2)),ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(14)

where Žā2=[Žā12,ŌĆ”,ŽāD2]ŌŖż\mbsigma^2 = [\sigma_1^2, \ldots, \sigma_D^2]^\topŽā2=[Žā12ŌĆŗ,ŌĆ”,ŽāD2ŌĆŗ]ŌŖż. The marginal covariance is C=WWŌŖż+diag(Žā2)\mbC = \mbW\mbW^\top + \diag(\mbsigma^2)C=WWŌŖż+diag(Žā2) ŌĆö still low-rank plus diagonal, but with DDD independent noise scales instead of one.

With the per-dimension prior:

p(W,Žā2)=ŌłÅd=1D[ŽćŌłÆ2(Žād2ŌłŻ╬Į0,Žā02)ŌĆēN(wdŌłŻ0,ŌĆēŽād2╬║0IM)],\begin{align} p(\mbW, \mbsigma^2) = \prod_{d=1}^D \left[ \chi^{-2}(\sigma_d^2 \mid \nu_0, \sigma_0^2)\, \cN(\mbw_d \mid \mbzero,\, \tfrac{\sigma_d^2}{\kappa_0} \mbI_M) \right], \end{align}p(W,Žā2)=d=1ŌłÅDŌĆŗ[ŽćŌłÆ2(Žād2ŌĆŗŌłŻ╬Į0ŌĆŗ,Žā02ŌĆŗ)N(wdŌĆŗŌłŻ0,╬║0ŌĆŗŽād2ŌĆŗŌĆŗIMŌĆŗ)],ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(15)

the Gibbs updates for each (wd,Žād2)(\mbw_d, \sigma_d^2)(wdŌĆŗ,Žād2ŌĆŗ) pair are identical to those for PPCA but with Žād2\sigma_d^2Žād2ŌĆŗ in place of the shared Žā2\sigma^2Žā2. All rows are independent given {zn}\{\mbz_n\}{znŌĆŗ}, so they can be updated in parallel.

Other Linear Latent Variable Models┬Č

Independent Components Analysis┬Č

ICA replaces the Gaussian latent prior with a product of non-Gaussian marginals:

znŌł╝iidp(z)=ŌłÅm=1Mp(zm),xnŌłŻznŌł╝N(Wzn+╬╝,ŌĆģŌĆŖdiag(Žā2)).\begin{align} \mbz_n &\iid{\sim} p(\mbz) = \prod_{m=1}^M p(z_m), \\ \mbx_n \mid \mbz_n &\sim \cN(\mbW \mbz_n + \mbmu,\; \diag(\mbsigma^2)). \end{align}znŌĆŗxnŌĆŗŌłŻznŌĆŗŌĆŗŌł╝iidp(z)=m=1ŌłÅMŌĆŗp(zmŌĆŗ),Ōł╝N(WznŌĆŗ+╬╝,diag(Žā2)).ŌĆŗŌĆŗ
(16)

The non-Gaussian prior is essential: if p(z)p(\mbz)p(z) were Gaussian, any correlation in p(z)p(\mbz)p(z) could be absorbed into W\mbWW, reducing the model to factor analysis. A common choice is a Laplace (heavy-tailed) prior, p(zm)ŌłØeŌłÆŌłŻzmŌłŻp(z_m) \propto e^{-|z_m|}p(zmŌĆŗ)ŌłØeŌłÆŌłŻzmŌĆŗŌłŻ, which encourages the latent factors to be sparse.

ICA is widely used in signal processing (e.g., separating audio sources) and neuroscience.

Probabilistic Canonical Correlation Analysis┬Č

Suppose we observe two data modalities, xnŌłłRDx\mbx_n \in \reals^{D_x}xnŌĆŗŌłłRDxŌĆŗ and ynŌłłRDy\mby_n \in \reals^{D_y}ynŌĆŗŌłłRDyŌĆŗ. Probabilistic CCA posits:

zn(s)Ōł╝iidN(0,IMs),zn(x)Ōł╝iidN(0,IMx),zn(y)Ōł╝iidN(0,IMy),xnŌł╝N(Wxxzn(x)+Wxszn(s)+╬╝x,ŌĆģŌĆŖŽā2IDx),ynŌł╝N(Wyyzn(y)+Wyszn(s)+╬╝y,ŌĆģŌĆŖŽā2IDy).\begin{align} \mbz_n^{(s)} &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_{M_s}), & \mbz_n^{(x)} &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_{M_x}), & \mbz_n^{(y)} &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_{M_y}), \\ \mbx_n &\sim \cN(\mbW_{xx}\mbz_n^{(x)} + \mbW_{xs}\mbz_n^{(s)} + \mbmu_x,\; \sigma^2 \mbI_{D_x}), \\ \mby_n &\sim \cN(\mbW_{yy}\mbz_n^{(y)} + \mbW_{ys}\mbz_n^{(s)} + \mbmu_y,\; \sigma^2 \mbI_{D_y}). \end{align}zn(s)ŌĆŗxnŌĆŗynŌĆŗŌĆŗŌł╝iidN(0,IMsŌĆŗŌĆŗ),Ōł╝N(WxxŌĆŗzn(x)ŌĆŗ+WxsŌĆŗzn(s)ŌĆŗ+╬╝xŌĆŗ,Žā2IDxŌĆŗŌĆŗ),Ōł╝N(WyyŌĆŗzn(y)ŌĆŗ+WysŌĆŗzn(s)ŌĆŗ+╬╝yŌĆŗ,Žā2IDyŌĆŗŌĆŗ).ŌĆŗzn(x)ŌĆŗŌł╝iidN(0,IMxŌĆŗŌĆŗ),zn(y)ŌĆŗŌł╝iidN(0,IMyŌĆŗŌĆŗ),ŌĆŗ
(17)

The shared latent variables zn(s)\mbz_n^{(s)}zn(s)ŌĆŗ capture common structure across modalities; private variables zn(x)\mbz_n^{(x)}zn(x)ŌĆŗ and zn(y)\mbz_n^{(y)}zn(y)ŌĆŗ capture modality-specific variation.

Bach & Jordan, 2005 showed that the MLE recovers the classical CCA solution: the shared weights Wxs\mbW_{xs}WxsŌĆŗ and Wys\mbW_{ys}WysŌĆŗ correspond to the left and right singular vectors of the sample cross-correlation matrix diag(Sxx)ŌłÆ1/2Sxydiag(Syy)ŌłÆ1/2\diag(\mbS_{xx})^{-1/2} \mbS_{xy} \diag(\mbS_{yy})^{-1/2}diag(SxxŌĆŗ)ŌłÆ1/2SxyŌĆŗdiag(SyyŌĆŗ)ŌłÆ1/2.

Conclusion┬Č

This chapter developed a family of linear Gaussian latent variable models with progressively richer structure:

ModelNoise covarianceLatent prior
PPCAŽā2I\sigma^2 \mbIŽā2I (isotropic)N(0,I)\cN(\mbzero, \mbI)N(0,I)
Factor analysisdiag(Žā2)\diag(\mbsigma^2)diag(Žā2) (diagonal)N(0,I)\cN(\mbzero, \mbI)N(0,I)
ICAdiag(Žā2)\diag(\mbsigma^2)diag(Žā2)ŌłÅmp(zm)\prod_m p(z_m)ŌłÅmŌĆŗp(zmŌĆŗ) (non-Gaussian)
PCCAŽā2I\sigma^2 \mbIŽā2I (per modality)shared + private Gaussians

Key takeaways:

  • PCA is the MLE for PPCA: the principal components are the leading eigenvectors of the sample covariance, computed efficiently via SVD.

  • The posterior on latent variables requires only inverting an M├ŚMM \times MM├ŚM system (not D├ŚDD \times DD├ŚD), making inference tractable.

  • Gibbs sampling for PPCA / FA reduces to iterated Bayesian linear regressions ŌĆö one per output dimension.

  • The non-Gaussian prior in ICA breaks the rotational indeterminacy of FA and enables recovery of independent source signals.

Next Steps

  • Variational Autoencoders ŌĆö the nonlinear generalization: replace the linear decoder with a neural network

  • Linear Dynamical Systems ŌĆö PPCA extended to sequential data; the Kalman filter is exact inference in a linear Gaussian SSM

Recommended Reading

Bishop, 2006, Ch 12.

References┬Č
  1. Bach, F. R., & Jordan, M. I. (2005). A probabilistic interpretation of canonical correlation analysis.
  2. Bishop, C. M. (2006). Pattern recognition and machine learning. Springer.
